CIND820 Big Data Analytics Project - EDA Data Mining for Customer Behavior Analysis in Retail Sales¶

In [1]:
# import libraries 

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.dates as mdates
In [2]:
# import dataset

path = r"C:\Users\jrroz\Desktop\June\TMU\Course Materials\CIND820\Data\Online Retail.xlsx"

df = pd.read_excel(path)

1. Data Summary and Structure¶

1.1 High-Level Inspection¶

  • High-level view of dataset: view head and tail
  • Check count of unique values
  • Check data types
  • Check start and end dates
In [3]:
df.info()

In [5]:
# count of unique values

num_rows = df.shape[0]
num_cols = df.shape[1]
num_customers = df['CustomerID'].nunique()
num_products = df['StockCode'].nunique()
num_invoices = df['InvoiceNo'].nunique()
num_countries = df['Country'].nunique()

print(num_rows, num_cols, num_customers, num_products, num_invoices, num_countries)

541909 8 4372 4070 25900 38

1.2 Quantitative Variables - Boxplots¶

  • Check boxplots of key variables (Quantity, UnitPrice, Revenue)
1.2.1 Quantity¶
In [10]:
# unfiltered

plt.boxplot(df['Quantity'], vert=False)
plt.title("Quantity")
plt.show()
# filtered - cancellations and bad debt excluded

filtered = df.loc[
    (~df["InvoiceNo"].str.startswith("C", na=False)) &
    (~df["InvoiceNo"].str.startswith("A", na=False)),
    "Quantity"
]

plt.boxplot(filtered.dropna(), vert=False)
plt.title("Quantity (Valid Sales Only)")
plt.show()
# filtered - cancellations and bad debt excluded
# outliers excluded

plt.boxplot(filtered.dropna(), vert=False, showfliers=False)
plt.title("Quantity (Valid Sales Only) (Outliers Excl.)")
plt.show()

  • There are negative values for Quantity, likely representing cancellations or returns.

  • In valid sales (excluding cancellations and bad debt adjustments), there are some extreme outliers — a few transactions have Quantity > 70,000.

  • Even after removing cancellations, bad debt adjustments, and outliers, some negative Quantity values remain, likely due to returns.

  • Around 50% of transactions have Quantity between 1 and 10, with a median of approximately 3–4.

1.2.2 Unit Price¶
In [11]:
# unfiltered

plt.boxplot(df['UnitPrice'].dropna(), vert=False) 
plt.title('Unit Price')
plt.show()

# filtered - cancellations and bad debt excluded

filtered = df.loc[
    (df["UnitPrice"] > 0) &
    (~df["InvoiceNo"].str.startswith("C", na=False)) *
    (~df["InvoiceNo"].str.startswith("A", na=False)),
    "UnitPrice"
]

plt.boxplot(filtered.dropna(), vert=False)
plt.title("Unit Price (Valid Sales Only)")
plt.show()

# filtered - cancellations and bad debt excluded
# outliers excluded

plt.boxplot(filtered.dropna(), vert=False, showfliers=False)
plt.title("Unit Price (Valid Sales Only) (Outliers Excl.)")
plt.show()

  • The boxplot of all Unit Price values shows very large outliers, both negative and positive.

  • After excluding cancellations and bad debt, the boxplot still shows large outliers, though they are less extreme.

  • For valid sales with outliers removed, about 50% of transactions have a Unit Price between £1.60 and £4.10, with a median of approximately £2.00.

  • Most merchandise is low-priced, while a small number of items are very high-priced, ranging from hundreds to thousands.

1.2.3 Revenue¶
In [12]:
# create revenue column

df['Revenue'] = df['Quantity'] * df['UnitPrice']

# unfiltered

plt.boxplot(df['Revenue'].dropna(), vert=False) 
plt.title('Revenue Per Transaction')
plt.show()

# filtered - cancellations and bad debt excluded

filtered = df.loc[
    (df["Revenue"] > 0) &
    (~df["InvoiceNo"].str.startswith("C", na=False)) *
    (~df["InvoiceNo"].str.startswith("A", na=False)),
    "Revenue"
]

plt.boxplot(filtered.dropna(), vert=False)
plt.title("Revenue Per Transaction (Valid Sales Only)")
plt.show()

# filtered - cancellations and bad debt excluded
# outliers excluded

plt.boxplot(filtered.dropna(), vert=False, showfliers=False)
plt.title("Revenue Per Transaction(Valid Sales Only) (Outliers Excl.)")
plt.show()

  • Boxplots of all revenue show large outliers, likely due to adjustments, returns, credits, or miscellaneous expenses.

  • Valid sales still exhibit large outliers, primarily from high-priced or high-quantity transactions.

  • After excluding outliers from valid sales, about 50% of transactions have revenue between £4 and £17, with a median of approximately £10.

In [13]:
# total revenue

total_revenue = df['Revenue'].sum()

print(total_revenue)

9747747.933999998

1.3 Qualitative Variables¶

1.3.1 CustomerID (Transactions)¶
In [14]:
# check count

df["CustomerID"].value_counts().reset_index()
Out[14]:
CustomerID count
0 17841.0 7983
1 14911.0 5903
2 14096.0 5128
3 12748.0 4642
4 14606.0 2782
... ... ...
4367 17986.0 1
4368 13256.0 1
4369 18184.0 1
4370 13747.0 1
4371 15195.0 1

4372 rows × 2 columns

In [15]:
# average transactions per customer, std. dev

mean = df["CustomerID"].value_counts().mean()
stddev = df["CustomerID"].value_counts().std()

print("Average number of transactions per CustomerID:", mean, "\n" "Standard deviation: ", stddev)

# dataset is very skewed

Average number of transactions per CustomerID: 93.05329368709972 
Standard deviation:  232.4716077416496
In [16]:
# purchase frequency

purchase_frequency = df.groupby('CustomerID')['InvoiceNo'].nunique().sort_values(ascending=False)

plt.hist(purchase_frequency, bins=100)
plt.title("Customer Transaction Frequency Distribution")
plt.xlabel("Number of Transactions")
plt.ylabel("Number of Customers")
plt.show()

# with bins 

bins = [0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 50, 500]
labels = ['1-5', '6-10', '11-15', '16-20', '21-25', '26-50', '50+']

frequency_bins = pd.cut(purchase_frequency, bins=bins, labels=labels)

frequency_bins.value_counts().sort_index().plot(kind='bar')
plt.title("Customer Transaction Frequency Distribution")
plt.xlabel("Number of Transactions")
plt.ylabel("Number of Customers")
plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.show()

  • The distribution is highly right-skewed.

  • Vast majority of customers have 1 to 5 transactions, with the number of customers declining steeply beyond 6 transactions.

In [17]:
frequency_bins.value_counts().sort_index()
Out[17]:
InvoiceNo
1-5      3285
6-10      628
11-15     220
16-20      97
21-25      42
26-50      76
50+        24
Name: count, dtype: int64
In [18]:
# revenue per customer

revenue_per_customer = df.groupby('CustomerID')['Revenue'].sum().sort_values(ascending=False)

# Top customers by revenue

revenue_per_customer.head(10)
Out[18]:
CustomerID
14646.0    279489.02
18102.0    256438.49
17450.0    187482.17
14911.0    132572.62
12415.0    123725.45
14156.0    113384.14
17511.0     88125.38
16684.0     65892.08
13694.0     62653.10
15311.0     59419.34
Name: Revenue, dtype: float64
In [19]:
# boxplot revenue per customer

plt.boxplot(revenue_per_customer.dropna(), vert=False)
plt.title("Revenue by Customer (Valid Sales Only)")
plt.show()

plt.boxplot(revenue_per_customer.dropna(), vert=False, showfliers=False)
plt.title("Revenue by Customer (Valid Sales Only) (Outliers Excl.)")
plt.show()

  • Revenue by customer (valid sales only) shows large outliers, with some customers generating hundreds of thousands in revenue.

  • A significant number of customers beyond the IQR generate thousands in revenue.

  • After removing outliers, about 50% of customers generated between £250 and £1,700 in revenue, with a median of approximately £600.

  • Some customers have large returns, shown by negative revenue values, which skews the data and lowers the average revenue per customer.

In [20]:
# proportion of revenue accounted by top 10 customers

top_10_revenue_prop = (revenue_per_customer.head(10).sum()/total_revenue)*100

print(f"The top 10 customers by revenue account for {top_10_revenue_prop.round(1)}% of the total revenue.")

The top 10 customers by revenue account for 14.0% of the total revenue.
1.3.2 Country¶
In [21]:
# Top countries by transaction count 

df['Country'].value_counts().head(10).plot(kind='bar')
plt.title("Top 10 Countries by Transactions")
plt.show()

# Top countries by transaction count (excl. UK)

df[df['Country'] != 'United Kingdom']['Country'].value_counts().head(10).plot(kind='bar')
plt.title("Top 10 Countries by Transactions (excl. UK)")
plt.show()

# Top countries by revenue 

top_countries_revenue = df.groupby('Country')['Revenue'].sum().sort_values(ascending=False)

top_countries_revenue.head(10).plot(kind='bar')
plt.title("Top 10 Countries by Revenue")
plt.xlabel("Country")
plt.show()

# Top countries by revenue (excl. UK)

top_countries_revenue = top_countries_revenue[top_countries_revenue.index != 'United Kingdom']

top_countries_revenue.head(10).plot(kind='bar')
plt.title("Top 10 Countries by Revenue (excl. UK)")
plt.xlabel("Country")
plt.show()

# Normal Transactions vs Cancellations

# df['Cancellation'].value_counts().plot(kind='bar')
# plt.title("Normal Transactions vs Cancellations")
# plt.show()

  • As a UK-based business, the United Kingdom accounts for the vast majority of total sales and transaction volume.

  • Outside the UK, Germany, France, and Ireland rank as the top three markets by transaction frequency.

  • Despite lower transaction counts than the top-3 countries mentioned above, the Netherlands generates higher total revenue, likely driven by bulk wholesale orders or higher-priced inventory.

1.3.3 StockCode & Description¶
In [22]:
# top merchandise by transactions, price, revenue, unit sales

valid_sales = df.loc[(df['UnitPrice'] > 0) & (df['Quantity'] > 0)]

# top merchandise by number of transactions 

valid_sales['StockCode'].value_counts().head(10).plot(kind='bar')
plt.title("Top 10 Products by Transactions")
plt.ylabel("Transaction Count")
plt.show()

# top merchandise by average price

top_products_price = valid_sales.groupby('StockCode')['UnitPrice'].mean().sort_values(ascending=False)

top_products_price.head(10).plot(kind='bar')
plt.title("Top 10 Products by Avg. Unit Price")
plt.ylabel('Unit Price')
plt.show()

# top merchandise by revenue

top_products_revenue = valid_sales.groupby('StockCode')['Revenue'].sum().sort_values(ascending=False)

top_products_revenue.head(10).plot(kind='bar')
plt.title("Top 10 Products by Revenue")
plt.ylabel("Revenue")
plt.show()

# top merchandise by unit sales

top_products_units = valid_sales.groupby('StockCode')['Quantity'].sum().sort_values(ascending=False)

top_products_units.head(10).plot(kind='bar')
plt.title("Top 10 Products by Units Solds")
plt.ylabel("Unit Count")
plt.show()
  • The dataset contains many miscellaneous transactions, such as Amazon fees, rebates, postage fees, etc., which should be considered and accounted for during analysis.
In [23]:
# table of products that appear in above graphs

# transactions

top_products_transactions_list = valid_sales['StockCode'].value_counts().head(10).index.tolist()

# price

top_products_price_list = top_products_price.head(10).index.tolist()

# revenue

top_products_revenue_list = top_products_revenue.head(10).index.tolist()

# units

top_products_units_list = top_products_units.head(10).index.tolist()

# combined 

top_products_combined_list = top_products_transactions_list + top_products_price_list + top_products_revenue_list + top_products_units_list

top_products_combined_descriptions = df.loc[df['StockCode'].isin(top_products_combined_list)]

top_products_combined_descriptions[['StockCode', 'Description']].value_counts()

2. Data Quality Check¶

In [28]:
# flag bad debt fields and cancellations

df['BadDebt'] = df['InvoiceNo'].str.contains(r"^A", na=False)
df['Cancellation'] = df['InvoiceNo'].str.contains(r"^C", na=False)

In [31]:
a = df.loc[df['InvoiceNo'].str.startswith("A", na = False), :] # bad debt
b = df.loc[df['InvoiceNo'].str.startswith("C", na = False), :] # cancellations

# Summary

print(f"There are {a.shape[0]} fields with bad debt adjustments.")
print(f"There are {b.shape[0]} fields with cancellations.")
print(f"There are {noncancel_negquantity.shape[0]} fields with negative quantities that are not cancellations")

There are 3 fields with bad debt adjustments.
There are 9288 fields with cancellations.
There are 1336 fields with negative quantities that are not cancellations

# Consider removing fields with UnitPrice == 0 during analysis?

df.isna().sum()

df['Description_NA'].value_counts().plot(kind='bar')

plt.show()

2.4 Identify Real Sales¶

In [38]:
# flag returns

df['IsReturn'] = df['Quantity'] < 0
In [39]:
# check StockCodes that begin with a letter

df['IsStockCodeLetter'] = df['StockCode'].str.isalpha()

Alpha_StockCodes = df.loc[df['IsStockCodeLetter'] == True, ['StockCode', 'Description']]

Alpha_StockCodes.value_counts()
Out[39]:
StockCode  Description               
POST       POSTAGE                       1252
DOT        DOTCOM POSTAGE                 709
M          Manual                         571
D          Discount                        77
S          SAMPLES                         63
AMAZONFEE  AMAZON FEE                      34
CRUK       CRUK Commission                 16
DCGSSGIRL  GIRLS PARTY BAG                 13
DCGSSBOY   BOYS PARTY BAG                  11
PADS       PADS TO MATCH ALL CUSHIONS       4
B          Adjust bad debt                  3
m          Manual                           1
Name: count, dtype: int64
In [40]:
# remove POST, DOT, M, D, S, AMAZONFEE, CRUK, B, m from StockCodes during analysis

not_real_product = ['POST', 'DOT', 'M', 'D', 'S', 'AMAZONFEE', 'CRUK', 'B', 'm']

df['RealProduct'] = ~df['StockCode'].isin(not_real_product)

df.loc[df['RealProduct'] == False].head()
Out[40]:
InvoiceNo StockCode Description Quantity InvoiceDate UnitPrice CustomerID Country Revenue BadDebt Cancellation CustomerID_NA Description_NA IsReturn IsStockCodeLetter RealProduct

141 C536379 D Discount -1 2010-12-01 09:41:00 27.50 14527.0 United Kingdom -27.50 False True False False True True False
386 536403 POST POSTAGE 1 2010-12-01 11:27:00 15.00 12791.0 Netherlands 15.00 False False False False False True False
1123 536527 POST POSTAGE 1 2010-12-01 13:04:00 18.00 12662.0 Germany 18.00 False False False False False True False
1814 536544 DOT DOTCOM POSTAGE 1 2010-12-01 14:32:00 569.77 NaN United Kingdom 569.77 False False True False False True False
In [41]:
# check sum of discounts

total_discounts = df.loc[df['StockCode'] == 'D']['Revenue'].sum()

print(total_discounts)

# about $5700 in discounts - likely negligible during analysis

-5696.219999999999

3. Temporal Analysis¶

3.1 Daily Aggregation¶

3.1.1 Aggregate Data - Daily¶
In [42]:
# check InvoiceDate

df['InvoiceDate'].dtype
Out[42]:
dtype('<M8[ns]')
In [43]:
# real sales (non negative quantity and price, no returns, real products)

real_sales = df.loc[(df['BadDebt'] == False) &
                    (df['Cancellation'] == False) &
                    (df['IsReturn'] == False) &
                    (df['RealProduct'] == True)].copy()
In [44]:
# aggregate data by day

aggregate_day = df.groupby(df['InvoiceDate'].dt.date).agg({
    'InvoiceNo': 'count',  # number of transactions
    'Revenue': 'sum'       # total revenue
}).rename(columns={'InvoiceNo': 'Transactions'})

# real sales

aggregate_day_real_sales = real_sales.groupby(real_sales['InvoiceDate'].dt.date).agg({
    'InvoiceNo': 'count',  # number of transactions
    'Revenue': 'sum'       # total revenue
}).rename(columns={'InvoiceNo': 'Transactions'})
3.1.2 Plot Data - Daily¶
In [45]:
# daily transaction volume (all data)

aggregate_day.plot(
    y=['Transactions','Revenue'], 
    figsize=(14,6),
    title='Daily Transactions and Revenue (All Data)',
    color=['blue', 'green'])
plt.xlabel('Date')
plt.ylabel('Count / Revenue')
plt.legend()
plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.show()

# daily transaction volume (real sales)

aggregate_day_real_sales.plot(
    y=['Transactions','Revenue'], 
    figsize=(14,6),
    title='Daily Transactions and Revenue (Real Sales)',
    color=['blue', 'green'])
plt.xlabel('Date')
plt.ylabel('Count / Revenue')
plt.legend()
plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.show()

  • Filtering for "real sales" (removing fees, returns, and misc. transactions) significantly reduces volatility, providing a clearer view of underlying consumer patterns.

  • The uncleaned dataset exhibits much more extreme peaks and troughs, largely driven by administrative adjustments, bulk cancellations, and misc. fees.

  • A prominent spike in sales is visible during the holiday season (Q4), marking the most active period for the business.

  • The "real sales" view reveals a massive revenue spike on the final day of the dataset, which may warrant further investigation into specific large-scale transactions.

  • A primary drawback of this analysis is the one-year timeframe, which prevents the comparison of year-over-year growth or the verification of long-term seasonal consistency.

3.2 Weekly Aggregation¶

3.2.1 Aggregate Data - Weekly¶
In [46]:
# aggregate data by week

aggregate_week = df.resample('W', on='InvoiceDate').agg({
    'InvoiceNo' : 'count',
    'Revenue' : 'sum'
}).rename(columns={'InvoiceNo' : 'Transactions'})

# real data

aggregate_week_real_sales = real_sales.resample('W', on='InvoiceDate').agg({
    'InvoiceNo' : 'count',
    'Revenue' : 'sum'
}).rename(columns={'InvoiceNo' : 'Transactions'})
3.2.2 Plot Data - Weekly¶
In [47]:
# weekly transaction volume (all data)

aggregate_week.plot(
    y=['Transactions','Revenue'], 
    figsize=(14,6),
    title='Weekly Transactions and Revenue (All Data)',
    color=['blue', 'green'],
    marker='o')
plt.xlabel('Week')
plt.ylabel('Count / Revenue')
plt.legend()
plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.show()

# weekly transaction volume (real sales)

aggregate_week_real_sales.plot(
    y=['Transactions','Revenue'], 
    figsize=(14,6),
    title='Weekly Transactions and Revenue (Real Sales)',
    color=['blue', 'green'],
    marker='o')
plt.xlabel('Week')
plt.ylabel('Count / Revenue')
plt.legend()
plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.show()

  • Analyzing revenue on a weekly basis reveals a positive growth trend that is otherwise obscured by the volatility of daily data.

  • The number of transactions remains relatively consistent throughout the year, showing a slight positive inclination, indicating a stable and slowly expanding customer base.

  • There is a sharp, near-total decline in revenue from mid-December 2010 to early January 2011. While transactions declined slightly, the lack of revenue during this window likely stems from the business closing for the holiday period, a pause in processing payments/shippings, or a data anomalie (error, return, fees, etc.).

3.3 Monthly Aggregation¶

3.3.1 Aggregate Data - Monthly¶
In [48]:
# aggregate data by month

aggregate_month = df.resample('ME', on='InvoiceDate').agg({
    'InvoiceNo' : 'count',
    'Revenue' : 'sum'
}).rename(columns={'InvoiceNo' : 'Transactions'})

# real data

aggregate_month_real_sales = real_sales.resample('ME', on='InvoiceDate').agg({
    'InvoiceNo' : 'count',
    'Revenue' : 'sum'
}).rename(columns={'InvoiceNo' : 'Transactions'})
3.3.2 Plot Data - Monthly¶
In [49]:
# monthly transaction volume (all data)

aggregate_month.plot(
    y=['Transactions','Revenue'], 
    figsize=(14,5),
    title='Monthly Transactions and Revenue (All Data)',
    color=['blue', 'green'],
    marker='o')
plt.xlabel('Month')
plt.ylabel('Count / Revenue')
plt.legend()
plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.show()

# monthly transaction volume (real sales)

aggregate_month_real_sales.plot(
    y=['Transactions','Revenue'], 
    figsize=(14,5),
    title='Monthly Transactions and Revenue (Real Sales)',
    color=['blue', 'green'],
    marker='o')
plt.xlabel('Month')
plt.ylabel('Count / Revenue')
plt.legend()
plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.show()

  • Monthly trends are smoother compared to daily and weekly trends, making overall patterns easier to observe.

  • December 2011 shows lower revenue and transaction counts, likely due to the incomplete month of data.

  • Sales tend to ramp up in the months leading up to the holiday season.

  • Sales decline in the months following the holiday season, reflecting seasonal fluctuations.

3.4 Day-of-Week Analysis¶

3.4.1 Aggregate Data - Day of Week¶
In [50]:
# create Day of Week column

df['DayOfWeek'] = df['InvoiceDate'].dt.day_name()
real_sales['DayOfWeek'] = real_sales['InvoiceDate'].dt.day_name()

# aggregate data

dayofweek = df.groupby('DayOfWeek').agg({
    'InvoiceNo' : 'count',
    'Revenue' : 'sum'
}).rename(columns={'InvoiceNo' : 'Transactions'}) 

# real sales

dayofweek_real_sales = real_sales.groupby('DayOfWeek').agg({
    'InvoiceNo' : 'count',
    'Revenue' : 'sum'
}).rename(columns={'InvoiceNo' : 'Transactions'}) 

# reorder days

days_order = ['Monday','Tuesday','Wednesday','Thursday','Friday','Saturday','Sunday']

dayofweek = dayofweek.reindex(days_order)
dayofweek_real_sales = dayofweek_real_sales.reindex(days_order)
3.4.2 Plot Data - Day of Week¶
In [51]:
# transaction volume and revenue by day of week (all)

dayofweek.plot(
    y=['Transactions','Revenue'], 
    figsize=(14,5),
    title='Day of Week Aggregation - Transactions and Revenue (All Data)',
    color=['blue', 'green'],
    marker='o')
plt.xlabel('Day of Week')
plt.ylabel('Count / Revenue')
plt.legend()
plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.show()

# transaction volume and revenue by day of week (real sales)

dayofweek_real_sales.plot(
    y=['Transactions','Revenue'], 
    figsize=(14,5),
    title='Day of Week Aggregation - Transactions and Revenue (Real Sales)',
    color=['blue', 'green'],
    marker='o')
plt.xlabel('Day of Week')
plt.ylabel('Count / Revenue')
plt.legend()
plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.show()

  • The dataset contains zero recorded sales on Saturdays, strongly suggesting that the store is closed or does not process any transactions on that day.

  • Revenue consistently peaks on Thursdays before experiencing a steep decline through Friday and into the weekend.

  • While revenue fluctuates throughout the week, transaction volume remains relatively stable, reaching its lowest point on Sundays.

  • There is a marginal discrepancy between "all data" and "real sales" at the weekly level, indicating that administrative fees and returns are distributed fairly evenly and do not significantly skew the weekly performance trends.

3.5 Hourly Analysis¶

3.5.1 Aggregate Daily - Hourly¶
In [52]:
# create Hour column

df['Hour'] = df['InvoiceDate'].dt.hour
real_sales['Hour'] = real_sales['InvoiceDate'].dt.hour

# aggregate data by hour

hourly = df.groupby('Hour').agg({
    'InvoiceNo' : 'count',
    'Revenue' : 'sum'
}).rename(columns={'InvoiceNo' : 'Transactions'})

# real sales

hourly_real_sales = real_sales.groupby('Hour').agg({
    'InvoiceNo' : 'count',
    'Revenue' : 'sum'
}).rename(columns={'InvoiceNo' : 'Transactions'})
3.5.2 Plot Data - Hourly¶
In [53]:
# transaction volume and revenue by day of week (all)

hourly.plot(
    y=['Transactions','Revenue'], 
    figsize=(14,5),
    title='Hourly Aggregation - Transactions and Revenue (All Data)',
    color=['blue', 'green'],
    marker='o')
plt.xlabel('Hour')
plt.ylabel('Count / Revenue')
plt.legend()
plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.show()

# transaction volume and revenue by day of week (real sales)

hourly_real_sales.plot(
    y=['Transactions','Revenue'], 
    figsize=(14,5),
    title='Hourly Aggregation - Transactions and Revenue (Real Sales)',
    color=['blue', 'green'],
    marker='o')
plt.xlabel('Hour')
plt.ylabel('Count / Revenue')
plt.legend()
plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.show()

# sales peak at noon
# peak period is from 10:00 to 15:00

  • The highest volume of transactions occurs between 10:00 and 15:00, representing the core business hours for customer engagement.

  • Sales typically peak at noon, suggesting a midday surge in ordering activity, potentially coinciding with lunch-break shopping.

  • Transaction volume is minimal before 08:00 and drops significantly after 15:00, mirroring standard business hours in the UK.

3.6 Rolling Temporal Analysis¶

3.6.1 Aggregate Data - Rolling 7-day/30-day Average¶
In [54]:
# create rolling averages

aggregate_day['Revenue_7d'] = aggregate_day['Revenue'].rolling(window=7).mean()
aggregate_day['Revenue_30d'] = aggregate_day['Revenue'].rolling(window=30).mean()

aggregate_day['Transactions_7d'] = aggregate_day['Transactions'].rolling(window=7).mean()
aggregate_day['Transactions_30d'] = aggregate_day['Transactions'].rolling(window=30).mean()

# real sales

aggregate_day_real_sales['Revenue_7d'] = aggregate_day_real_sales['Revenue'].rolling(window=7).mean()
aggregate_day_real_sales['Revenue_30d'] = aggregate_day_real_sales['Revenue'].rolling(window=30).mean()

aggregate_day_real_sales['Transactions_7d'] = aggregate_day_real_sales['Transactions'].rolling(window=7).mean()
aggregate_day_real_sales['Transactions_30d'] = aggregate_day_real_sales['Transactions'].rolling(window=30).mean()
3.6.2 Plot Data - Rolling 7-day/30-day Averages¶
In [55]:
# revenue trends

aggregate_day[['Revenue', 'Revenue_7d', 'Revenue_30d']].plot(
    figsize=(14,6),
    title='Revenue Trends with Rolling Averages (All Data)',
    color=['lightgray', 'red', 'blue']
)
plt.xlabel('Date')
plt.ylabel('Revenue')

plt.gca().xaxis.set_major_locator(mdates.MonthLocator())
plt.gca().xaxis.set_major_formatter(mdates.DateFormatter('%b %Y'))
plt.xticks(rotation=45)

plt.grid(True, linestyle=':')
plt.show()

# transaction trends

aggregate_day[['Transactions', 'Transactions_7d', 'Transactions_30d']].plot(
    figsize=(14,6),
    title='Transaction Trends with Rolling Averages (All Data)',
    color=['lightgray', 'red', 'blue']
)
plt.xlabel('Date')
plt.ylabel('Number of Transactions')

plt.gca().xaxis.set_major_locator(mdates.MonthLocator())
plt.gca().xaxis.set_major_formatter(mdates.DateFormatter('%b %Y'))
plt.xticks(rotation=45)

plt.grid(True, linestyle=':')
plt.show()

# revenue trends (real sales)

aggregate_day_real_sales[['Revenue', 'Revenue_7d', 'Revenue_30d']].plot(
    figsize=(14,6),
    title='Revenue Trends with Rolling Averages (Real Sales)',
    color=['lightgray', 'red', 'blue']
)
plt.xlabel('Date')
plt.ylabel('Revenue')

plt.gca().xaxis.set_major_locator(mdates.MonthLocator())
plt.gca().xaxis.set_major_formatter(mdates.DateFormatter('%b %Y'))
plt.xticks(rotation=45)

plt.grid(True, linestyle=':')
plt.show()

# transaction trends (real sales)

aggregate_day_real_sales[['Transactions', 'Transactions_7d', 'Transactions_30d']].plot(
    figsize=(14,6),
    title='Transaction Trends with Rolling Averages (Real Sales)',
    color=['lightgray', 'red', 'blue']
)
plt.xlabel('Date')
plt.ylabel('Number of Transactions')

plt.gca().xaxis.set_major_locator(mdates.MonthLocator())
plt.gca().xaxis.set_major_formatter(mdates.DateFormatter('%b %Y'))
plt.xticks(rotation=45)

plt.grid(True, linestyle=':')
plt.show()

  • While raw data is highly volatile, analyzing "real sales" (filtered for returns and fees) reveals clearer underlying patterns. Transaction counts and revenue generally follow the same trajectory.

  • A 7-day rolling average highlights a monthly cycle where activity peaks mid-month. This behavior is potentially linked to pay day cycles.

  • The 30-day rolling average confirms a powerful seasonal trend. Sales ramp up significantly in the months preceding the holiday season (September–November) and decline sharply in the following months.

  • While transaction volume shows stronger spikes, revenue growth is more steady. Larger revenue increases are concentrated in the pre-holiday period, suggesting higher-value orders during this time.

Annual Performance Dips:

  • Mid-February & July/August: These periods represent the lowest transaction points of the year.

  • A notable pickup occurs from mid-February to mid-May, likely driven by major retail events (Valentine's Day, Easter, Mother's Day).

  • Following mid-May, transactions dip again through mid-August before rising rapidly as the fourth quarter approaches.
